summary: DeVault introduces Himitsu, a new Unix keyring and password manager inspired by Plan 9's factotum. At its core, Himitsu is a generic key/value store with a simple text-based IPC protocol, where credentials are stored as arbitrary key/value pairs with a convention system driven by 'proto' keys. The system emphasizes user consent for secret disclosure, zero-configuration credential discovery for applications, and extensibility through standardized protocols for both querying and prompting. Written in Hare with XChaCha20+Poly1305 encryption, it already has a Firefox add-on, GTK frontend, and SSH agent integration. DeVault envisions future uses including FDE boot integration, cross-device key synchronization, and datacenter service discovery.
key_points: Himitsu stores secrets as generic key/value pairs with a protocol convention system, giving it flexibility beyond traditional password managers
The simple text-based IPC protocol enables an ecosystem of frontends and integrations without tight coupling to any specific use case
User consent is built into the architecture — secrets are hidden by default and require explicit disclosure requests
Zero-configuration credential discovery lets applications like mail clients find their own connection details from the keystore
The encrypted index improves on tools like pass by not storing the list of entries in plain text
money_quotes: Himitsu draws inspiration from Plan 9's factotum, but polished up and redesigned for Unix.
Himitsu has no built-in knowledge of web logins, it just stores arbitrary keys and values.
You'll notice that the password is hidden here. In order to obtain it, we must ask for the user's consent.
Himitsu-aware software can discover your credentials and connection details without any additional configuration.
You could also use Himitsu for service discovery — imagine a key ring running on your datacenter LAN with entries for your Postgres database, SMTP credentials, and so on.
This offers an improvement over pass, for example, by not storing the list of entries in plain text.
Alright, back to kernel hacking. I got multi-tasking working yesterday!
key_insight: A credential store designed around generic key/value pairs and simple text protocols can serve as a universal foundation for secret management, service discovery, and zero-configuration application setup across Unix systems.
